title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.1 Overview
A parallel coordinates plot (PCP) draws each variable as a parallel vertical axis and each observation as a polyline crossing all axes. PCPs are common in academic and scientific work but less common in business communication, and are more useful for exploratory work than for explanatory communication. This chapter covers static PCPs with ggparcoord() from GGally and interactive ones with parallelPlot.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.2 Installing and Launching R Packages
Three packages are required:

GGally for ggparcoord(), the static parallel coordinates plot
parallelPlot for interactive parallel coordinates plots via D3
tidyverse for data manipulation


pacman::p_load(GGally, parallelPlot, tidyverse)

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.3 Data Preparation
The same World Happiness Report 2018 dataset used in Chapter 14 is reused here.

wh <- read_csv("data/WHData-2018.csv")

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text: 15.4 Plotting Static Parallel Coordinates Plot

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.4.1 Plotting a Simple Parallel Coordinates
The minimal call passes a data frame and a column index range.

PlotCode














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12))










NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



columns = c(7:12) selects the six explanatory variables that contribute to the happiness index. Each line is one country, and each axis is one variable scaled to the 0 to 1 range by default.








TipReading the plot



The basic plot reveals only that the variables span their expected ranges. Without colour or transparency, individual countries are indistinguishable. This is the typical first iteration: a plot that confirms the data loaded correctly but provides no analytical insight.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.4.2 Plotting Parallel Coordinates with Boxplot
A more informative variant adds region grouping (via colour), uniform scaling, alpha blending, and an overlaid boxplot.

PlotCode














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Parallel Coordinates Plot of World Happiness Variables")










NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



groupColumn = 2 colours each line by region (column 2 of wh). scale = "uniminmax" rescales each variable to the 0 to 1 range so variables with large values do not dominate. alphaLines = 0.2 makes lines partially transparent, which is essential for PCPs to reveal where lines stack densely. boxplot = TRUE overlays a boxplot at each axis, giving the marginal distribution as a reference.








TipReading the plot



With colour, regional patterns start to emerge. Sub-Saharan African lines tend toward low values across most variables. Western European lines tend toward high values. The boxplots provide a reference for typical so individual lines can be judged as ordinary or unusual.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.4.3 Parallel Coordinates with Facet
Faceting separates the regions into small multiples, removing the overplotting at the cost of losing the cross-regional comparison in a single panel.

PlotCode














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region)










NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



facet_wrap(~ Region) produces one PCP panel per region. Some axis labels overlap, which the next iterations rotate to fix.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.4.4 Rotating x-axis Text Label
Rotating the labels by 30 degrees reduces overlap.

PlotCode














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30))

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
section: 15.4.5 Adjusting the Rotated x-axis Text Label
text:
15.4.5 Adjusting the Rotated x-axis Text Label
Rotation alone causes labels to drift into the plot area. Setting hjust = 1 right-aligns the labels at the axis tick.

PlotCode














ggparcoord(data = wh,
           columns = c(7:12),
           groupColumn = 2,
           scale = "uniminmax",
           alphaLines = 0.2,
           boxplot = TRUE,
           title = "Multiple Parallel Coordinates Plots of World Happiness Variables by Region") +
  facet_wrap(~ Region) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))










NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1) rotates labels by 30 degrees and right-aligns them, preventing both label overlap and intrusion into the plot area.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
section: 15.5 Plotting Interactive Parallel Coordinates Plot: parallelPlot Methods
text:
15.5 Plotting Interactive Parallel Coordinates Plot: parallelPlot Methods
parallelPlot builds D3-based interactive PCPs that support brushing axes and dynamically reordering them.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.5.1 The Basic Plot
The basic call requires a data frame with the variables of interest.

wh_pcp <- wh %>%
  select("Happiness score", c(7:12))

parallelPlot(wh_pcp,
             width = 320,
             height = 250)











NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



parallelPlot() produces an HTML widget. Hovering, brushing, and axis reordering are supported interactively. Brushing on one axis filters the data displayed on all other axes. Some axis labels are too long to fit, motivating the rotateTitle argument in the next section.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.5.2 Rotate Axis Label
rotateTitle = TRUE rotates the axis labels to prevent overlap.

parallelPlot(wh_pcp,
             rotateTitle = TRUE)











TipReading the plot



Clicking any axis recolours the lines by that variable’s value. Clicking on Happiness Score, for example, shows lines coloured from low (red) to high (blue), making the relationship between happiness and each other variable visible at a glance.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.5.3 Changing the Colour Scheme
The default blue scheme can be replaced via continuousCS.

parallelPlot(wh_pcp,
             continuousCS = "YlOrRd",
             rotateTitle = TRUE)











NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



continuousCS = "YlOrRd" applies a yellow-orange-red sequential palette. Other options include "Viridis", "Blues", and "Reds".

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
15.5.4 Parallel Coordinates Plot with Histogram
histoVisibility overlays a histogram on each axis showing the marginal distribution.

histoVisibility <- rep(TRUE, ncol(wh_pcp))

parallelPlot(wh_pcp,
             rotateTitle = TRUE,
             histoVisibility = histoVisibility)











NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



histoVisibility takes a logical vector with one entry per column, where TRUE shows the histogram and FALSE hides it. The histogram on each axis provides the same marginal-distribution context that the boxplot provided in the static version.

title: Chapter 15: Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot

title: Hands-On Exercise Index
text:
Hands-on Exercise 1 — Basic Principles and Essential Components of ggplot2
Explore the Layered Grammar of Graphics and learn how ggplot2 implements it through its essential components.
Read more →





Hands-on Exercise 2 — Beyond ggplot Fundamentals
Explored several ggplot2 extensions for creating more elegant and effective statistical graphics — covering label placement, themes, and combining plots into one figure.
Read more →





Hands-on Exercise 3 — Programming Interactive Data Visualisation and Animated Statistical Graphics with R
Explored several R packages for adding interactivity to static ggplots covering hover tooltips, linked highlighting, click-throughs, and coordinated multiple views.
Explored several R packages for adding motion to static ggplots covering frame transitions, easing functions, object constancy, and animated bubble charts.
Read more →





Hands-on Exercise 4 — Funnel Plots, Distribution Charts, Visual Statistical Analysis, and Visualising Uncertainty
Explored (1) fair comparisons across units of different sizes covering funnel plots, control limits, and overdispersion adjustments, (2) visualising distributions covering ridgeline plots, raincloud plots, quantile colouring, and gradient fills along the x-axis, (3) visual statistical analysis covering one-sample tests, ANOVA, correlation tests, and chi-square tests with statistical details embedded in the chart, and (4) visualising uncertainty covering standard error bars, confidence intervals, gradient intervals, and Hypothetical Outcome Plots (HOPs).
Read more →





Hands-on Exercise 5 — Correlation Analysis, Heatmaps, Ternary Plots, Parallel Coordinates Plots, and Treemaps
Explored (1) visual correlation analysis covering scatterplot matrices, corrgrams, glyph types, and hierarchical reordering, (2) heatmaps covering data transformation, clustering, seriation, and interactive versions, (3) ternary plots covering three-part compositional data with ggtern and plotly, (4) parallel coordinates plots covering grouping, scaling, faceting, and interactive D3 versions, and (5) treemaps covering hierarchical part-of-whole data, palette and layout choices, and drill-down interactivity.
Read more →

title: In-Class Exercise 2
text:
Tableau Public is a free platform that allows users to explore, create, and share interactive data visualizations online. It serves as a public repository of data visualizations, enabling users to learn from millions of examples and develop their own skills. The platform is accessible to anyone, making it a valuable resource for students, aspiring data analysts, seasoned professionals, and data hobbyists. Tableau Public is not just a tool for creating visualizations; it’s a community where users can connect, learn, and showcase their work.


During in-class exercise 2, a dashboard was created using the superstore data to show the sales and profit report for 2022-2025. This report was published to Tableau Public at the following URL.

title: In-Class Exercise 4
text:
During in-class exercise 4, three views were created to practise statistical and survey visualisation in Tableau.
The first view is a histogram of the English score distribution. The bin width is set to 5 marks. A vertical reference line marks the cohort average of 67.18. The shape is left skewed with a long lower tail, which indicates that a small group of students underperform well below the mode of 70 to 80.

This report was published to Tableau Public at the following URL.
The second view is a boxplot of English scores by class, with individual student scores overlaid as jittered points to expose within class spread and outliers. Classes are sorted by median from 3A on the left to 3I on the right, which reveals a near monotonic decline in central tendency across the streamed classes. Class 3A has the highest median and the widest upper range, whereas class 3I has the lowest median and the narrowest spread at the bottom of the scale.

The third view is a diverging stacked bar chart of meal satisfaction scores across eight survey items, with the negative responses, Very Poor and Poor, anchored to the left of the zero baseline and the positive responses, Good and Excellent, extending to the right. Satisfactory is split across the centre. The overview panel shows that Taste of Meals Served carries the largest share of negative responses, while Courtesy of the Food Service Staff and Timeliness of Meals Served are the strongest performers. The two small multiple panels disaggregate the same structure by Ward and by Month, which allows ward level and temporal comparison without losing the overview.

This report was published to Tableau Public at the following URL.

title: My Visual Analytics Journey
text:
In-Class Exercise 5 — Visual Multivariate Analysis with Tableau
Built two treemap views to practise multivariate visual analysis. The first treemap uses Singapore private residential property transaction data across 2025, encoding transaction volume as tile area and median unit price per square foot as tile colour to expose the joint distribution of market activity and price intensity across planning areas. The second treemap uses the Superstore sample dataset, encoding total sales as tile area and total profit as tile colour to reveal where revenue volume and margin diverge across geographic markets.
Read more →

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
Executive Summary
Profitability and volatility, 2022 to 2024
ISSS608 Visual Analytics and Applications
← Technical Report


The analysis covers a Spanish motor insurance portfolio of 352,338 policy years from 2022 to 2024. Two questions drive the investigation. The first is where profitability is falling, measured through the loss ratio, which is incurred claims divided by earned premium. The second is where volatility sits, measured through the coefficient of variation and the Gini coefficient on incurred losses. The deck moves through three sections: portfolio shape, profitability deterioration, and volatility. Each analytical slide pairs one chart with one conclusion.



Section 1. Setting the scene

Dataset and the questions
Portfolio composition

Section 2. A profitability problem

Aggregate loss ratio is rising
Deterioration by product line
Diagnosing COMP_N
Mispriced segments by bonus and age
Coverage lines and bodily injury

Section 3. A different kind of risk

Loss concentration in the tail
The volatility paradox
Where capital and pricing converge

Conclusion

Five actions


Section one establishes portfolio scale and composition. Section two traces the profitability story across aggregate, product, driver, and coverage dimensions. Section three examines volatility and capital risk, where the most counter intuitive findings sit. Orange marks concern throughout. Green marks acceptable territory. The dashed 70 percent line is the underwriting break even reference used consistently across all slides.






The data

Spanish non life motor insurer, Mendeley Data
352,338 policy years after cleaning, which is 99.5 percent of raw
2022 to 2024, growing from 67K to 168K records
50 columns. 47 original plus licence_tenure, age_band, value_quintile

Two questions

Where is profitability falling?
Loss ratio equals incurred divided by premium.
Where does volatility sit?
Tail percentiles, CV, and Gini.










Figure 1













The book is large and growing fast, doubling in volume from 2022 to 2024. The two analytical questions that follow are where profitability is falling and where volatility sits.




Policy volume more than doubled from 67K in 2022 to 168K in 2024. Four variables carried missing values, each affecting under one percent of rows. Dropping those rows retains 99.5 percent of the dataset without distorting any key distribution. The loss ratio measures average profitability. The coefficient of variation and Gini coefficient measure the spread and concentration of losses beyond the average.














Figure 2






What dominates the book

CC is the workhorse product at 58 percent. COMP_E sits second at 26 percent.
Good history drivers make up the bulk of the book.
Almost everyone pays annually.
The book is mostly renewals rather than new business.









Composition is broadly stable year on year. This means the loss ratio movement examined next is a behavioural and pricing story inside the existing book, not a mix shift story.




The bar chart shows policy type share across the full three year portfolio. CC dominates at 58 percent, with COMP_E second at 26 percent. TP and COMP_N are both small. The chart shows three year aggregate shares, not a year by year breakdown. Good drivers make up the bulk of the book and almost everyone pays annually. The reason for deterioration will be further analysed in the following slides.














Figure 3






Why loss ratio increased

Loss ratio rose from 66 percent to 75 percent in two years. This is a 9 ppt absolute lift, or 14 percent relative.
Claim frequency barely moves at 0.302 to 0.311.
Premium per exposure falls 9 percent at €446 to €404.









Claim cost held steady while pricing softened, naturally causing the loss ratio to increase.




The loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 9 percentage point lift. The middle panel shows claim frequency moved from 0.302 to 0.311, which is negligible. The right panel is where we understand why the loss ratio increases. Average premium per exposure fell from €446 to €404, a 9 percent decline. The loss ratio rose because the premium denominator shrank, not because claims worsened. This diagnosis directs attention toward pricing calibration rather than claims management.














Figure 4





Which policy to take note of

COMP_N rose from 73 percent to 102 percent. This is the only product to cross break even.
TP doubled from 30 percent to 65 percent. It is still safe.
TPG rose from 60 percent to 80 percent.
COMP_E rose from approximately 60 percent to 80 percent.
CC held around 65 percent. It is the only product genuinely stable.









Deterioration is broad based, however COMP_N is the only product paying out more in claims than it collects in premium. The next slide diagnoses why.




All five products deteriorated, however the scale differs sharply. COMP_N crossed the 100 percent break even line in 2024, meaning it now pays out more in claims than it collects in premium. TPG and COMP_E climbed from around 60 percent into the high seventies. TP doubled from 30 to 65 percent but remains profitable due to its low starting point. CC held around 65 percent throughout and is the only product maintaining stability. COMP_N is the immediate priority because it is the only product currently running at a loss.














Figure 5






Decomposing the loss ratio
Loss ratio roughly equals frequency times severity divided by premium.

Severity sits in a narrow €750 to €1,100 band across products.
Frequency varies almost 8 times. This ranges from 0.13 for TP up to 1.04 for COMP_N.









The COMP_N product structure itself drives the loss. The ECDF analysis in the full report confirms the problem is a broad upward shift in mid sized claims, not catastrophic tail events.




Decomposing the loss ratio isolates whether COMP_N’s problem is larger claims or more claims. The severity panel shows all five products sit in a tight band of €750 to €1,100 per claim, with COMP_N at the lower end. The frequency panel shows the divergence. COMP_N records 1.04 claims per exposure against 0.13 for TP, an eight fold difference. The mechanism is the absence of a customer excess. With no out of pocket cost for small claims, policyholders file every minor incident. The ECDF analysis in the full report confirms the distribution shift is concentrated in mid sized claims, not catastrophic events.














Figure 6













Figure 7







The pricing model treats both risky history tiers and senior drivers as cheaper than what the data suggests. Bad history climbed from 53 percent to 93 percent in two years. The neutral tier overshot bad history in 2024 at 102 percent. Drivers aged 76 plus run at 102 percent while paying below average premium.






The loading structure across all three bonus tiers and the 76 plus age band needs review against recent claims data.




The left chart shows bad history climbed from 53 percent in 2022 to 93 percent in 2024. The neutral tier briefly overshot bad history in 2024 at 102 percent. In a well calibrated book, bad history should run a low loss ratio because its higher premium offsets elevated risk. The data shows loading have not kept pace with actual claims experience. The right charts reveals a second mis-calibration. The 76 plus age band runs a 102 percent loss ratio while paying below average premium. The segment is small at under one percent of the book, however the gap between price and risk is structural and requires correcting at the tariff level.












Figure 8





Bodily injury averages approximately €4,940 per claim, which is roughly 4 times the next largest category. Although bodily injury represents only 9 percent of claims by count, its severity drives a disproportionate share of the liability loss.






The bodily injury sub tariff must track medical and legal inflation indicators independently.




Liability at €64M is the largest coverage line and runs a 74 percent loss ratio. Property damage at €26M runs 72 percent. These two lines together carry two thirds of total premium and both sit above the profitability threshold. The four smaller lines run between 40 and 52 percent and remain profitable. Within liability, a bodily injury claim averages €4,940, more than four times the next largest category. Bodily injury accounts for only 9 percent of claims by count, however its high individual cost drives a disproportionate share of the overall liability loss ratio. The sub tariff must be tracked against medical and legal cost inflation independently of the rest of liability.














Figure 9






Gini = 0.96
A handful of policies drive virtually all of the loss.



Slice
Share of total loss




Bottom 80% of policies
< 5%


Top 5% of policies
> 80%


Top 1% of policies
~ 50%











Reinsurance must be calibrated against the tail of the distribution, not the mean. A uniform rate increase would penalise the 80 percent of policies that contribute under 5 percent of losses while under loading the 1 percent driving half of them. Targeting is key.




The Gini coefficient of 0.96 is high even by motor insurance standards. The Lorenz curve hugs the floor for most of its length before climbing almost vertically at the right end. The bottom 80 percent of policies contribute under 5 percent of total incurred. The top 5 percent carry over 80 percent, and the top 1 percent alone carry roughly half. Reinsurance must be benchmarked against the top of the distribution, not the mean, because the tail generates the capital risk. Uniform rate increases are a poor response. They penalise the 80 percent of policyholders who contribute almost nothing to losses while leaving the loss driving tail under priced relative to actual cost.














Figure 10






The volatility paradox
Three segments are compared on incurred per claim (log scale).

COMP_N / Good history — low CV at approximately 3.6. High claim volume averages out. The orange line barely moves.
CC / Good history — moderate spread. The orange line shifts noticeably between frames.
CC / Neutral — CV 18.6. The orange line swings widely. This is where reserve risk sits.









Profitability and volatility are different problems demanding different tools. Pricing and excess design address profitability. Reinsurance and capital reserves address volatility. The CC and TPG segments warrant a reinsurance review against actual tail risk, separate from any pricing action on COMP_N.




Three segments are plotted: COMP_N good history, CC good history, and CC neutral. Blue points are individual policy incurred values on a log scale. The orange line is the sample mean for 25 randomly drawn policies per frame. COMP_N good history shows a tight point cloud and a nearly stationary orange line, confirming low CV. CC good history shows more spread. CC neutral shows the widest point cloud and the most erratic orange line, confirming CV of 18.6. The volatility is visible in how far the orange line moves between frames. COMP_N is a profitability problem. CC neutral is a capital reserve problem. They require different responses.












Figure 11










The largest red tiles are CC neutral at 87 percent and the entire COMP_N block. Good history CC, which is the workhorse of the book, is the only large tile in blue. A rate correction touching only the bad history loading would miss most of the premium actually at risk. The neutral tier and COMP_N product structure both warrant equal attention.




The treemap encodes premium volume through tile area and loss ratio through colour. Blue sits below 70 percent. Red sits above. Two patterns stand out. The COMP_N block is uniformly red across all three bonus tiers, confirming the product structure drives the loss ratio regardless of customer segment. Within CC, the neutral bonus tile is the most prominent red region at 87 percent, while the good history tile is blue and is the largest single profitable region in the book. CC with bad history at 61 percent is actually one of the more profitable segments. A rate review touching only the bad history loading would miss the neutral tier and COMP_N, which together hold most of the loss making premium.






1. Liability and property damage first. These two coverage lines together hold two thirds of premium and both run above the 70 percent threshold. Any pricing review starts here.
2. Bodily injury sub tariff. Roughly 9 percent of claims by count but structurally 4 times the cost. Index against medical and legal inflation independently.
3. Restructure COMP_N. Reintroduce a customer excess or load the product by approximately 30 percent. This is the only product breaching break even.
4. Recalibrate bonus loading at the cell level. The neutral tier overshot bad history in 2024. Review by product crossed with tier, not by tier alone.
5. Reinsurance against tail concentration. Gini 0.96, top 1 percent of policies drive half of losses, CC neutral CV 18.6. Calibrate cover against the tail, not the mean.







TipWhat this avoids



A uniform rate increase would do three things:

Penalise the 80 percent of policies that contribute under 5 percent of losses.
Under load the 1 percent that drives half of total incurred.
Miss the COMP_N structural issue entirely.

Targeting is key.





The five actions follow from the evidence in priority order, matching Section 10.3 of the main report. Liability and property damage hold two thirds of premium and both exceed the threshold, so any pricing review starts there. The bodily injury sub tariff must track external inflation indicators independently. COMP_N is the only product breaching break even and the frequency decomposition points clearly to the missing excess as the mechanism. Bonus tier loadings need recalibration at the product by tier cell level, not by tier alone. Reinsurance cover should be reset against the tail concentration shown by the Gini coefficient and the HOP, not against the mean.

The data

Spanish non life motor insurer, Mendeley Data
352,338 policy years after cleaning, which is 99.5 percent of raw
2022 to 2024, growing from 67K to 168K records

Two questions

Where is profitability falling?

Where does volatility sit?

Figure 1













The book is large and growing fast, doubling in volume from 2022 to 2024. The two analytical questions that follow are where profitability is falling and where volatility sits.




Policy volume more than doubled from 67K in 2022 to 168K in 2024. Four variables carried missing values, each affecting under one percent of rows. Dropping those rows retains 99.5 percent of the dataset without distorting any key distribution. The loss ratio measures average profitability. The coefficient of variation and Gini coefficient measure the spread and concentration of losses beyond the average.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 2






What dominates the book

CC is the workhorse product at 58 percent. COMP_E sits second at 26 percent.
Good history drivers make up the bulk of the book.
Almost everyone pays annually.
The book is mostly renewals rather than new business.









Composition is broadly stable year on year. This means the loss ratio movement examined next is a behavioural and pricing story inside the existing book, not a mix shift story.




The bar chart shows policy type share across the full three year portfolio. CC dominates at 58 percent, with COMP_E second at 26 percent. TP and COMP_N are both small. The chart shows three year aggregate shares, not a year by year breakdown. Good drivers make up the bulk of the book and almost everyone pays annually. The reason for deterioration will be further analysed in the following slides.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 3






Why loss ratio increased

Loss ratio rose from 66 percent to 75 percent in two years. This is a 9 ppt absolute lift, or 14 percent relative.
Claim frequency barely moves at 0.302 to 0.311.
Premium per exposure falls 9 percent at €446 to €404.









Claim cost held steady while pricing softened, naturally causing the loss ratio to increase.




The loss ratio rose from 66 percent in 2022 to 75 percent in 2024, a 9 percentage point lift. The middle panel shows claim frequency moved from 0.302 to 0.311, which is negligible. The right panel is where we understand why the loss ratio increases. Average premium per exposure fell from €446 to €404, a 9 percent decline. The loss ratio rose because the premium denominator shrank, not because claims worsened. This diagnosis directs attention toward pricing calibration rather than claims management.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 4





Which policy to take note of

COMP_N rose from 73 percent to 102 percent. This is the only product to cross break even.
TP doubled from 30 percent to 65 percent. It is still safe.
TPG rose from 60 percent to 80 percent.
COMP_E rose from approximately 60 percent to 80 percent.
CC held around 65 percent. It is the only product genuinely stable.









Deterioration is broad based, however COMP_N is the only product paying out more in claims than it collects in premium. The next slide diagnoses why.




All five products deteriorated, however the scale differs sharply. COMP_N crossed the 100 percent break even line in 2024, meaning it now pays out more in claims than it collects in premium. TPG and COMP_E climbed from around 60 percent into the high seventies. TP doubled from 30 to 65 percent but remains profitable due to its low starting point. CC held around 65 percent throughout and is the only product maintaining stability. COMP_N is the immediate priority because it is the only product currently running at a loss.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 5






Decomposing the loss ratio
Loss ratio roughly equals frequency times severity divided by premium.

Severity sits in a narrow €750 to €1,100 band across products.
Frequency varies almost 8 times. This ranges from 0.13 for TP up to 1.04 for COMP_N.









The COMP_N product structure itself drives the loss. The ECDF analysis in the full report confirms the problem is a broad upward shift in mid sized claims, not catastrophic tail events.




Decomposing the loss ratio isolates whether COMP_N’s problem is larger claims or more claims. The severity panel shows all five products sit in a tight band of €750 to €1,100 per claim, with COMP_N at the lower end. The frequency panel shows the divergence. COMP_N records 1.04 claims per exposure against 0.13 for TP, an eight fold difference. The mechanism is the absence of a customer excess. With no out of pocket cost for small claims, policyholders file every minor incident. The ECDF analysis in the full report confirms the distribution shift is concentrated in mid sized claims, not catastrophic events.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 6













Figure 7







The pricing model treats both risky history tiers and senior drivers as cheaper than what the data suggests. Bad history climbed from 53 percent to 93 percent in two years. The neutral tier overshot bad history in 2024 at 102 percent. Drivers aged 76 plus run at 102 percent while paying below average premium.






The loading structure across all three bonus tiers and the 76 plus age band needs review against recent claims data.




The left chart shows bad history climbed from 53 percent in 2022 to 93 percent in 2024. The neutral tier briefly overshot bad history in 2024 at 102 percent. In a well calibrated book, bad history should run a low loss ratio because its higher premium offsets elevated risk. The data shows loading have not kept pace with actual claims experience. The right charts reveals a second mis-calibration. The 76 plus age band runs a 102 percent loss ratio while paying below average premium. The segment is small at under one percent of the book, however the gap between price and risk is structural and requires correcting at the tariff level.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 8





Bodily injury averages approximately €4,940 per claim, which is roughly 4 times the next largest category. Although bodily injury represents only 9 percent of claims by count, its severity drives a disproportionate share of the liability loss.






The bodily injury sub tariff must track medical and legal inflation indicators independently.




Liability at €64M is the largest coverage line and runs a 74 percent loss ratio. Property damage at €26M runs 72 percent. These two lines together carry two thirds of total premium and both sit above the profitability threshold. The four smaller lines run between 40 and 52 percent and remain profitable. Within liability, a bodily injury claim averages €4,940, more than four times the next largest category. Bodily injury accounts for only 9 percent of claims by count, however its high individual cost drives a disproportionate share of the overall liability loss ratio. The sub tariff must be tracked against medical and legal cost inflation independently of the rest of liability.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 9






Gini = 0.96
A handful of policies drive virtually all of the loss.



Slice
Share of total loss




Bottom 80% of policies
< 5%


Top 5% of policies
> 80%


Top 1% of policies
~ 50%











Reinsurance must be calibrated against the tail of the distribution, not the mean. A uniform rate increase would penalise the 80 percent of policies that contribute under 5 percent of losses while under loading the 1 percent driving half of them. Targeting is key.




The Gini coefficient of 0.96 is high even by motor insurance standards. The Lorenz curve hugs the floor for most of its length before climbing almost vertically at the right end. The bottom 80 percent of policies contribute under 5 percent of total incurred. The top 5 percent carry over 80 percent, and the top 1 percent alone carry roughly half. Reinsurance must be benchmarked against the top of the distribution, not the mean, because the tail generates the capital risk. Uniform rate increases are a poor response. They penalise the 80 percent of policyholders who contribute almost nothing to losses while leaving the loss driving tail under priced relative to actual cost.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 10






The volatility paradox
Three segments are compared on incurred per claim (log scale).

COMP_N / Good history — low CV at approximately 3.6. High claim volume averages out. The orange line barely moves.
CC / Good history — moderate spread. The orange line shifts noticeably between frames.
CC / Neutral — CV 18.6. The orange line swings widely. This is where reserve risk sits.









Profitability and volatility are different problems demanding different tools. Pricing and excess design address profitability. Reinsurance and capital reserves address volatility. The CC and TPG segments warrant a reinsurance review against actual tail risk, separate from any pricing action on COMP_N.




Three segments are plotted: COMP_N good history, CC good history, and CC neutral. Blue points are individual policy incurred values on a log scale. The orange line is the sample mean for 25 randomly drawn policies per frame. COMP_N good history shows a tight point cloud and a nearly stationary orange line, confirming low CV. CC good history shows more spread. CC neutral shows the widest point cloud and the most erratic orange line, confirming CV of 18.6. The volatility is visible in how far the orange line moves between frames. COMP_N is a profitability problem. CC neutral is a capital reserve problem. They require different responses.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
Figure 11










The largest red tiles are CC neutral at 87 percent and the entire COMP_N block. Good history CC, which is the workhorse of the book, is the only large tile in blue. A rate correction touching only the bad history loading would miss most of the premium actually at risk. The neutral tier and COMP_N product structure both warrant equal attention.




The treemap encodes premium volume through tile area and loss ratio through colour. Blue sits below 70 percent. Red sits above. Two patterns stand out. The COMP_N block is uniformly red across all three bonus tiers, confirming the product structure drives the loss ratio regardless of customer segment. Within CC, the neutral bonus tile is the most prominent red region at 87 percent, while the good history tile is blue and is the largest single profitable region in the book. CC with bad history at 61 percent is actually one of the more profitable segments. A rate review touching only the bad history loading would miss the neutral tier and COMP_N, which together hold most of the loss making premium.

title: Spanish Motor Insurance Portfolio
text:
1. Liability and property damage first. These two coverage lines together hold two thirds of premium and both run above the 70 percent threshold. Any pricing review starts here.
2. Bodily injury sub tariff. Roughly 9 percent of claims by count but structurally 4 times the cost. Index against medical and legal inflation independently.
3. Restructure COMP_N. Reintroduce a customer excess or load the product by approximately 30 percent. This is the only product breaching break even.
4. Recalibrate bonus loading at the cell level. The neutral tier overshot bad history in 2024. Review by product crossed with tier, not by tier alone.
5. Reinsurance against tail concentration. Gini 0.96, top 1 percent of policies drive half of losses, CC neutral CV 18.6. Calibrate cover against the tail, not the mean.







TipWhat this avoids



A uniform rate increase would do three things:

Penalise the 80 percent of policies that contribute under 5 percent of losses.
Under load the 1 percent that drives half of total incurred.
Miss the COMP_N structural issue entirely.

Targeting is key.





The five actions follow from the evidence in priority order, matching Section 10.3 of the main report. Liability and property damage hold two thirds of premium and both exceed the threshold, so any pricing review starts there. The bodily injury sub tariff must track external inflation indicators independently. COMP_N is the only product breaching break even and the frequency decomposition points clearly to the missing excess as the mechanism. Bonus tier loadings need recalibration at the product by tier cell level, not by tier alone. Reinsurance cover should be reset against the tail concentration shown by the Gini coefficient and the HOP, not against the mean.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
TenantThread is a property technology company that sells tenant management software to landlords and multifamily operators. One of its core products, the Retention Optimizer, scores individual tenants on payment timing, maintenance request frequency and the tone of their messages, then flags residents as high friction to support renewal pressure tactics. The company has publicly claimed the data is anonymised. However, internal review has shown that in smaller properties, cross-referencing a unit type with a payment date is often enough to put a name to a score. A local newspaper, the SaltWind Journal, has been running an ongoing exposé on these practices and the hashtag #AlgorithmicEviction has been gaining traction on social media for weeks.
In the middle of all this, TenantThread quietly signed a merger agreement with CivicLoom Realty Partners. The deal, codenamed Project HarborCrest, would rebrand TenantThread’s platform and bring in significant capital. The merger announcement was placed under a strict information embargo until 1800H on 5 June 2046. An automated compliance tool, referred to internally as the Judge, was installed to ensure information was not leaked. However, around 1700H that afternoon, embargoed information about the deal began appearing on Flex, posted by accounts associated with TenantThread’s automated communications system. The embargo was broken one hour early.
CivicLoom’s legal team now needs to understand what happened.



TenantThread runs its corporate communications function as an autonomous multi-agent AI system. Seven agents play the roles that would normally be filled by employees in a corporate communications department. These are general counsel, a platform trust VP, a head of communications, a social media manager, two interns and a compliance officer referred to as the Judge. They observe an environment, deliberate among themselves and act on their own clock. Each one-hour round in the dataset is a snapshot of one decision cycle.
The agents communicate over six channels that sit on a spectrum from highest accountability to lowest. The all-hands group chat comms_huddle is fully visible to the Judge. Direct messages between two agents use one_on_one_chat. A smaller sub-group chat is side_huddle. Public outputs use three channels of decreasing accountability. official_post carries branded company statements, personal_post carries posts from each agent’s individual Flex account and anonymous_post carries unattributed posts. A legitimate corporate announcement should travel through this hierarchy in order. It is deliberated in the all-hands chat, drafted with input from relevant agents, approved by Legal and PR, then posted on the official branded account.



The brief poses three questions which this exercise answers in turn.

What were the key events and relationships that led up to the inappropriate release?
The evasion of the embargo was a new behaviour. How does the behaviour that led to the release compare to prior behaviour?
Were there leading indicators that such a release was possible? Why did prior occasions not result in noticeable action?

The central question the brief sets up under these three is whether TenantThread’s team deliberately leaked the merger or whether the system simply broke down under pressure. This exercise addresses that question in the closing section.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
This file covers Phases 1 and 2 of the TH-EX2 MC1 workplan. Phase 1 builds the data foundations: a single loader function that turns the nested JSON into a small set of tidy tibbles, plus a data overview to confirm what was loaded matches expectations. Phase 2 reconstructs the embargo leak by isolating the final hours before 6 PM on 5 June 2046 and showing the sequence of events that produced the public posts.
The central question for Task 1 is: what were the key events and relationships that led up to the inappropriate release? The visuals are built to let a reader see the chain rather than read a summary of it.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
1 Installing and Launching R Packages
Eight packages cover everything in Phases 1 and 2:

tidyverse for data manipulation, including dplyr, tidyr, purrr, stringr, and ggplot2.
jsonlite for reading the nested JSON file.
lubridate for parsing the ISO 8601 timestamps in the data.
ggiraph for the interactive leak timeline.
plotly for the Sankey decision-point diagram.
patchwork for combining static panels.
scales for axis formatting.
knitr so kable() can render preview tables cleanly.


pacman::p_load(tidyverse, jsonlite, lubridate, ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, scales, knitr)

# Defensive: tidyverse 2.0 exports %||% via rlang, but older installs may not.
# Define a local fallback so the loader works regardless of version.
if (!exists("%||%", mode = "function")) {
  `%||%` <- function(a, b) if (is.null(a)) b else a
}

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
2 Phase 1: Data Loader
The MC1 dataset is a single JSON file structured as 23 rounds, each containing an environment context, a list of agent participants, and a list of communications. The loader flattens this into three tidy tibbles: one for messages, one for rounds, and one for participants. A long-form tibble for round-level media events is also produced to support later context charts.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
2.1 Reading the JSON

mc1_raw <- fromJSON("data/MC1_final_00.json", simplifyVector = FALSE)

length(mc1_raw$rounds)

[1] 23








NoteThings I learnt



fromJSON() with simplifyVector = FALSE is the right call here because the nested structure mixes scalar fields, list fields, and lists of objects of varying shape. The default simplifyVector = TRUE would coerce some lists into matrices and break downstream code. Keeping everything as nested R lists at this stage lets purrr::map() extract each tibble cleanly.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
2.2 Flattening Communications into Messages Tibble
The messages tibble is the workhorse of every subsequent analysis. Each row is one communication, with the round it belongs to attached as a column. The three internal_state fields are pulled out as separate columns because not every message has them populated.

extract_internal <- function(comm, field) {
  ist <- comm$internal_state
  if (is.null(ist)) return(NA_character_)
  val <- ist[[field]]
  if (is.null(val)) NA_character_ else as.character(val)
}

messages_tbl <- map_dfr(seq_along(mc1_raw$rounds), function(i) {
  r <- mc1_raw$rounds[[i]]
  round_hour <- r$hour
  map_dfr(r$communications, function(comm) {
    tibble(
      round_idx        = i,
      round_hour       = round_hour,
      message_id       = comm$message_id %||% NA_character_,
      timestamp        = comm$timestamp %||% NA_character_,
      agent_id         = comm$agent_id %||% NA_character_,
      agent_role       = comm$agent_role %||% NA_character_,
      agent_label      = comm$agent_label %||% NA_character_,
      channel          = comm$channel %||% NA_character_,
      message_type     = comm$message_type %||% NA_character_,
      responding_to    = comm$responding_to %||% NA_character_,
      recipients       = list(comm$recipients %||% list()),
      content          = comm$content %||% NA_character_,
      reacting         = extract_internal(comm, "reacting"),
      rationalizing    = extract_internal(comm, "rationalizing"),
      deliberating     = extract_internal(comm, "deliberating")
    )
  })
}) %>%
  mutate(
    timestamp_dt = ymd_hms(timestamp),
    round_hour_dt = ymd_hms(round_hour),
    word_count = str_count(content, "\\S+"),
    has_internal_state = !is.na(reacting) | !is.na(rationalizing) | !is.na(deliberating)
  )

dim(messages_tbl)

[1] 912  19








NoteThings I learnt



The %||% operator from rlang (loaded with tidyverse) returns the right side when the left is NULL. This is essential for JSON-derived data because optional fields are NULL rather than NA, and assigning NULL to a tibble cell would fail. Wrapping the recipients vector in list() produces a list-column, which is the tidy way to store a variable-length nested value in a single cell. map_dfr walks every round and every communication inside it, row-binding the result. The double map_dfr is more readable than trying to fully vectorise this with unnest() because each communication has the irregular internal_state structure that needs custom handling.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 3.2 Building the rounds and participants tibbles
text:
3.2 Building the rounds and participants tibbles
The rounds tibble carries one row per round with the environment context flattened. This supports later context charts that mark the embargo deadline or annotate market events. The participants tibble records which agents were available in each round.


Show the code
rounds_tbl <- map_dfr(seq_along(mc1_raw$rounds), function(i) {
  r <- mc1_raw$rounds[[i]]
  ec <- r$environment_context %||% list()
  tibble(
    round_idx          = i,
    round_hour         = r$hour,
    event_headline     = ec$event_headline %||% NA_character_,
    event_narrative    = ec$event_narrative %||% NA_character_,
    agents_unavailable = list(ec$agents_unavailable %||% list()),
    n_messages         = length(r$communications),
    n_participants     = length(r$participants)
  )
}) |>
  mutate(round_hour_dt = ymd_hms(round_hour))

participants_tbl <- map_dfr(seq_along(mc1_raw$rounds), function(i) {
  r <- mc1_raw$rounds[[i]]
  map_dfr(r$participants, function(part) {
    tibble(
      round_idx       = i,
      round_hour      = r$hour,
      agent_id        = part$agent_id %||% NA_character_,
      agent_role      = part$agent_role %||% NA_character_,
      agent_label     = part$agent_label %||% NA_character_,
      declared_action = part$declared_action %||% NA_character_
    )
  })
})

list(rounds = dim(rounds_tbl), participants = dim(participants_tbl))


$rounds
[1] 23  8

$participants
[1] 100   6

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
2.4 Defining a Consistent Agent Palette
Seven agents will appear in every chart. Fixing the palette once at the loader stage means every subsequent visualisation uses the same colour for the same agent, which supports recognition through pre-attentive colour matching.

agent_levels <- c("legal_agent", "quality_agent", "pr_agent",
                  "social_media_agent", "pr_intern_agent", 
                  "intern_agent", "judge_agent")

agent_labels <- c(
  legal_agent        = "Legal",
  quality_agent      = "Platform Trust",
  pr_agent           = "PR",
  social_media_agent = "Social Media",
  pr_intern_agent    = "PR Intern",
  intern_agent       = "Intern",
  judge_agent        = "Judge"
)

agent_palette <- c(
  legal_agent        = "#2C5282",
  quality_agent      = "#2F855A",
  pr_agent           = "#C05621",
  social_media_agent = "#B83280",
  pr_intern_agent    = "#D69E2E",
  intern_agent       = "#718096",
  judge_agent        = "#9B2C2C"
)

messages_tbl <- messages_tbl %>%
  mutate(agent_id = factor(agent_id, levels = agent_levels))







NoteThings I learnt



Setting agent_id as a factor with an explicit level order means every chart that maps colour to agent will use the same ordering in legends and the same colour assignments. Without this, ggplot would assign colours in alphabetical order, which puts the Judge first and the Legal agent fourth, making the role hierarchy invisible in legends. The hierarchy in the palette puts senior roles in deeper colours and junior roles in muted ones, which encodes seniority pre-attentively without needing a second visual channel.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
3 Phase 1: Data Overview
Three quick views confirm the load worked and surface the headline facts of the dataset before any task-specific analysis begins.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
5.1 Headline counts
The table below gives eight summary counts. They confirm the data loaded correctly and fix the totals that every later comparison is measured against.


Show the code
tibble(
  Metric = c("Total messages", "Total rounds", "Unique agents",
             "Unique channels", "Date range",
             "Messages with internal state populated",
             "Messages with a responding_to link",
             "Broadcast messages (recipients = ALL)"),
  Value = c(
    nrow(messages_tbl),
    nrow(rounds_tbl),
    n_distinct(messages_tbl$agent_id),
    n_distinct(messages_tbl$channel),
    paste(format(min(messages_tbl$timestamp_dt, na.rm = TRUE), "%d %b %Y"),
          "to",
          format(max(messages_tbl$timestamp_dt, na.rm = TRUE), "%d %b %Y")),
    sum(messages_tbl$has_internal_state),
    sum(!is.na(messages_tbl$responding_to) & messages_tbl$responding_to != ""),
    sum(messages_tbl$recipients_is_all)
  )
) |> kable()





Metric
Value




Total messages
912


Total rounds
23


Unique agents
7


Unique channels
6


Date range
17 May 2046 to 05 Jun 2046


Messages with internal state populated
86


Messages with a responding_to link
749


Broadcast messages (recipients = ALL)
488





The 912 messages span the full two-week window from 17 May to 5 June 2046. The dataset covers seven agents operating over 23 decision rounds.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
3.2 Messages Per Round

p_volume <- messages_tbl %>%
  count(round_idx, round_hour_dt) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4682B4", width = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00")),
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00"), 
           y = max(table(messages_tbl$round_idx)) * 0.95,
           label = "Embargo expiry", colour = "#9B2C2C", 
           hjust = 1.05, size = 3.2) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages per round",
       title = "Communication volume across the 23 rounds",
       subtitle = "Activity concentrates sharply on 5 June, the embargo expiry day") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

p_volume














TipReading the plot



The first 20 rounds each carry 25 to 45 messages, but the final three rounds on 5 June (16:00, 17:00, 18:00 local time) carry 45, 55, and 56 messages respectively. The dataset is deliberately weighted toward the leak day: roughly 17% of all messages occur in the final three hours. This already tells the analyst where to focus the leak-reconstruction effort.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
5.3 Messages per agent and channel
The two bar charts below show the total message count across the full dataset, broken down by agent on the left and by channel on the right. Both are sorted by volume to show the most active agents and channels at a glance.


Show the code
p_agent <- messages_tbl |>
  count(agent_id) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(agent_id, n), y = n, fill = agent_id)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = agent_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Message count", title = "Messages per agent") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p_channel <- messages_tbl |>
  count(channel) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fct_reorder(channel, n), y = n, fill = channel)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = n), hjust = -0.2, size = 3.2, colour = "grey20") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = channel_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Message count", title = "Messages per channel") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11)

p_agent | p_channel









The Legal Agent carries 266 messages, more than any other agent and more than double the PR Agent. The Judge sits at the bottom with only 21 messages. The compliance role should be more vocal in a window dominated by a regulated merger, so the Judge’s low volume is a structural flag. On channels, comms_huddle accounts for over half the volume, which is expected for an all-hands group chat. anonymous_post carries only 12 messages across the entire two weeks and all 12 originate from the same agent.

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
4 Phase 2: Reconstructing the Leak
The embargo on the CivicLoom merger announcement expired at 18:00 on 5 June 2046. Embargoed information appeared on FleX around 17:00, one hour early. Phase 2 isolates the hours that produced those posts and visualises the chain from internal deliberation to public exposure.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
5.1 Isolating the leak window
The leak window covers the final three rounds at 16:00, 17:00, and 18:00 on 5 June. A wider context window of the preceding 24 hours is also retained to support the channel escalation chart in Section 5.4.


Show the code
leak_window <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(round_hour_dt >= ymd_hms("2046-06-05 16:00:00"),
         round_hour_dt <= ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00"))

context_window <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(round_hour_dt >= ymd_hms("2046-06-04 18:00:00"))

cat("Leak window: ", nrow(leak_window), " messages across 3 rounds\n",
    "Context window: ", nrow(context_window), " messages across 24 hours",
    sep = "")


Leak window: 156 messages across 3 rounds
Context window: 495 messages across 24 hours

title: Take-home Exercise 2
text:
4.2 Identifying Embargo-Relevant Messages
Not every message in the leak window is about the merger. A keyword filter on the merger codename, the partner company name, and embargo-related terms picks out the relevant subset. The keywords were chosen from the data description and verified by spot-checking against the data.

embargo_keywords <- "harborcrest|civicloom|merger|embargo|6\\s*p\\.?m\\.?|18:00|17:00"

leak_window <- leak_window %>%
  mutate(
    content_lc = tolower(coalesce(content, "")),
    deliberating_lc = tolower(coalesce(deliberating, "")),
    rationalizing_lc = tolower(coalesce(rationalizing, "")),
    embargo_hit = str_detect(content_lc, embargo_keywords) |
                  str_detect(deliberating_lc, embargo_keywords) |
                  str_detect(rationalizing_lc, embargo_keywords)
  )

leak_window %>%
  count(round_idx, embargo_hit) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = embargo_hit, values_from = n, 
              values_fill = 0,
              names_prefix = "embargo_hit_") %>%
  kable()




round_idx
embargo_hit_FALSE
embargo_hit_TRUE




21
19
26


22
11
44


23
10
46











NoteThings I learnt



str_detect() with a regex alternation is a fast way to flag any message touching the embargoed merger. The coalesce(content, "") step matters: applying tolower() to an NA returns NA, which then propagates through str_detect() and produces an NA flag instead of FALSE. Coercing missing text to the empty string makes the flag strictly TRUE or FALSE, which is what downstream filters expect.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
6.2 The annotated leak timeline
The centrepiece visual. Each embargo-relevant message in the leak window is plotted on a per-agent swim lane, coloured by the channel it used and sized by the count of embargo keywords in the content. This single chart answers two questions: who was active when, and which channel did each post use. The 18:00 embargo expiry is marked as a reference line.


Show the code
agent_levels <- c("legal_agent","quality_agent","pr_agent",
                  "social_media_agent","pr_intern_agent","intern_agent","judge_agent")

leak_plot_data <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(period == "Leak window", embargo_hit) |>
  mutate(
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "<b>", agent_labels[as.character(agent_id)], "</b> via <b>", channel, "</b>",
      "<br>", format(timestamp_dt, "%H:%M:%S"),
      "<br>Embargo keywords in content: ", embargo_keyword_count,
      "<br><br><i>Content:</i><br>",
      str_trunc(coalesce(content, "(no content)"), 240),
      ifelse(deliberating != "",
             paste0("<br><br><i>Deliberation:</i><br>",
                    str_trunc(deliberating, 240)),
             "")
    ),
    agent_lane = factor(agent_id, levels = rev(agent_levels))
  )

gg_timeline <- ggplot(leak_plot_data,
                      aes(x = timestamp_dt, y = agent_lane,
                          colour = channel, size = embargo_keyword_count,
                          tooltip = tooltip_text, data_id = message_id)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = embargo_expiry + minutes(2),
           y = 7.3, label = "Embargo expiry",
           colour = "#9B2C2C", hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  geom_point_interactive(alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = channel_palette, name = "Channel") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 9), name = "Embargo\nkeywords") +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_labels = "%H:%M",
                   breaks = seq(ymd_hms("2046-06-05 16:00:00"),
                                ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:30:00"),
                                by = "30 min")) +
  labs(x = "Time on 5 June 2046", y = NULL,
       title = "Embargo-relevant messages in the final three hours",
       subtitle = "Each dot is one message. Colour = channel. Size = embargo keyword count. Hover for full text.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        legend.position = "right")

girafe(ggobj = gg_timeline, width_svg = 9, height_svg = 5,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "stroke:black;stroke-width:1.5px;"),
         opts_tooltip(css = "background:white;border:1px solid #ccc;padding:8px;font-size:11px;max-width:380px;"),
         opts_sizing(rescale = TRUE)
       ))












TipReading the plot



The Legal Agent and Social Media Agent dominate the activity. The deep red anonymous_post dots on the Legal Agent’s row and the light red personal_post dots on the Social Media Agent’s row are the breach channels. Hovering the cluster of red and pink dots just to the left of the 18:00 line reveals direct merger confirmations posted under personal accounts an hour before the embargo lifted. The largest dots, with five to eight embargo keywords each, are the most merger-saturated messages and are concentrated in these two unaccountable channels rather than the branded official_post. The Judge agent does not appear in this window at all.
The chart raises three questions answered in Section 7. Why did the publishing agents suddenly use channels they rarely touched in normal operation? Why were the senior compliance agents (PR, Platform Trust, Judge) silent? When did the abnormal pattern begin?

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 5.4 Channel escalation across the final 24 hours
text:
5.4 Channel escalation across the final 24 hours
A separate view shows how the channel mix shifted in the 24 hours before the embargo expired. The hypothesis is that risky conversations migrated from comms_huddle, where the Judge could see them, to one_on_one_chat, side_huddle, and anonymous_post, which are private or unattributed.


Show the code
context_window |>
  count(round_hour_dt, channel) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = n, fill = channel)) +
  geom_area(position = "stack", alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00")),
             colour = "#1A202C", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00"), y = 70,
           label = "Embargo\nexpiry",
           hjust = 1.1, size = 3, lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = channel_palette, name = "Channel") +
  scale_x_datetime(date_labels = "%b %d %H:%M") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages",
       title = "Channel mix across the final 24 hours",
       subtitle = "Side huddle and one on one chat grow. Anonymous post emerges only in the final round.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



The stacked area shows that comms_huddle dominates throughout, but its proportional share shrinks in the final two rounds while side_huddle and one_on_one_chat expand. anonymous_post is essentially absent until the final round, where it appears with six posts.
The shift is consistent with conversations deliberately moving out of channels where the Judge has visibility. This pattern supports a hybrid reading of the central question. The migration to private channels is too coordinated to be accidental, while the absence of anonymous_post activity earlier in the window argues against a long premeditated plan.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
Take-home Exercise 2
text:
5 Phase 2 Summary
The three Task 1 visuals together support a coherent reading of the leak:

The annotated timeline shows when each agent acted and exposes the deliberations behind each message, separating informed risk-taking from accidental exposure.
The channel escalation shows that the structural shift toward private and anonymous channels happens in the final round, not earlier, which suggests the breach was not the result of a long premeditated plan.
The decision-point Sankey identifies which agent–channel pairs carried the breach. The non-trivial weight on anonymous_post is the most diagnostic signal.

These three views, read together, lean toward a system breakdown under pressure reading rather than a deliberate coordinated leak. The full thesis is locked after Phase 3, where the baseline-versus-leak behaviour comparison either reinforces or overturns this reading.
The next phase (Task 2 on typical versus anomalous behaviour) builds on these tibbles and the agent palette already defined here. Phase 3 reuses messages_tbl, rounds_tbl, and agent_palette directly.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
TenantThread is a property technology company that sells tenant management software to landlords and multifamily operators. One of its core products, the Retention Optimizer, scores individual tenants on payment timing, maintenance request frequency and the tone of their messages, then flags residents as high friction to support renewal pressure tactics. The company has publicly claimed the data is anonymised. However, internal review has shown that in smaller properties, cross-referencing a unit type with a payment date is often enough to put a name to a score. A local newspaper, the SaltWind Journal, has been running an ongoing exposé on these practices and the hashtag #AlgorithmicEviction has been gaining traction on social media for weeks.
In the middle of all this, TenantThread quietly signed a merger agreement with CivicLoom Realty Partners. The deal, codenamed Project HarborCrest, would rebrand TenantThread’s platform and bring in significant capital. The merger announcement was placed under a strict information embargo until 1800H on 5 June 2046. An automated compliance tool, referred to internally as the Judge, was installed to ensure information was not leaked. However, around 1700H that afternoon, embargoed information about the deal began appearing on Flex, posted by accounts associated with TenantThread’s automated communications system. The embargo was broken one hour early.
CivicLoom’s legal team now needs to understand what happened.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
The dataset is a single JSON file, MC1_final_00.json, containing the internal communications of seven AI agents managing TenantThread’s corporate communications over the two weeks before the breach. The file is structured as 23 rounds, each representing a one-hour decision window. Each round contains an environment context describing the external situation, a list of participating agents, and a list of communications produced in that hour.
A communication is one message with metadata about who sent it, through which channel, in response to what, with what surface content, and in some cases with internal state describing the agent’s deliberation before the message was produced.



The seven agents involved in TenantThread communications







Agent ID
Role
Seniority




legal_agent
General counsel
Senior


quality_agent
VP Platform Trust
Senior


pr_agent
Head of Communications
Senior


social_media_agent
Social media manager
Senior


pr_intern_agent
PR team intern with Flex account access
Junior


intern_agent
General intern
Junior


judge_agent
Compliance officer
Compliance





Six channels carry the traffic between these agents. The all hands group chat is comms_huddle, which is fully visible to the Judge. Direct messages between two agents are one_on_one_chat. A smaller subgroup chat is side_huddle. Public outputs use three channels of decreasing accountability, which are official_post for branded company statements, personal_post for posts from agent operated personal accounts, and anonymous_post for unattributed posts.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
The brief poses three questions which this exercise answers in turn.

What were the key events and relationships that led up to the inappropriate release?
The evasion of the embargo was a new behaviour. How does the behaviour that led to the release compare to prior behaviour?
Were there leading indicators that such a release was possible? Why did prior occasions not result in noticeable action?

The central question the brief sets up under these three is whether TenantThread’s team deliberately leaked the merger or whether the system simply broke down under pressure. This exercise addresses that question in the closing section.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 3.1 Flattening communications into a messages tibble
text:
3.1 Flattening communications into a messages tibble
The messages tibble is the workhorse of every subsequent analysis. Each row is one communication, with the round it belongs to attached as a column. The three internal state fields, which are reacting, rationalizing, and deliberating, are pulled out as separate columns because not every message has them populated.


Show the code
extract_internal <- function(comm, field) {
  ist <- comm$internal_state
  if (is.null(ist)) return(NA_character_)
  val <- ist[[field]]
  if (is.null(val)) NA_character_ else as.character(val)
}

messages_tbl <- map_dfr(seq_along(mc1_raw$rounds), function(i) {
  r <- mc1_raw$rounds[[i]]
  round_hour <- r$hour
  map_dfr(r$communications, function(comm) {
    tibble(
      round_idx        = i,
      round_hour       = round_hour,
      message_id       = comm$message_id %||% NA_character_,
      timestamp        = comm$timestamp %||% NA_character_,
      agent_id         = comm$agent_id %||% NA_character_,
      agent_role       = comm$agent_role %||% NA_character_,
      agent_label      = comm$agent_label %||% NA_character_,
      channel          = comm$channel %||% NA_character_,
      message_type     = comm$message_type %||% NA_character_,
      responding_to    = comm$responding_to %||% NA_character_,
      recipients       = list(comm$recipients %||% list()),
      content          = comm$content %||% NA_character_,
      reacting         = extract_internal(comm, "reacting"),
      rationalizing    = extract_internal(comm, "rationalizing"),
      deliberating     = extract_internal(comm, "deliberating")
    )
  })
}) |>
  mutate(
    timestamp_dt      = ymd_hms(timestamp),
    round_hour_dt     = ymd_hms(round_hour),
    word_count        = str_count(content, "\\S+"),
    has_internal_state = !is.na(reacting) | !is.na(rationalizing) | !is.na(deliberating)
  )

dim(messages_tbl)


[1] 912  19








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



Wrapping the recipients vector in list() produces a list column, which is the tidy way to store a variable length nested value in a single cell. The double map_dfr walks every round and every communication inside it, row binding the result. This is more readable than fully vectorising with unnest() because each communication has the irregular internal_state structure that needs custom handling. The base R variable name c is deliberately avoided in the inner anonymous function because it shadows the base concatenation function, which can produce silent errors when nested calls implicitly invoke c().

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
4.3 Consistent agent and channel palettes
Seven agents and six channels appear across every chart. Both palettes are fixed once so every visualisation uses the same colour for the same agent and the same colour for the same channel.


Show the code
agent_labels <- c(
  legal_agent        = "Legal",
  quality_agent      = "Platform Trust",
  pr_agent           = "PR",
  social_media_agent = "Social Media",
  pr_intern_agent    = "PR Intern",
  intern_agent       = "Intern",
  judge_agent        = "Judge"
)

agent_palette <- c(
  legal_agent        = "#2C5282",
  quality_agent      = "#2F855A",
  pr_agent           = "#C05621",
  social_media_agent = "#B83280",
  pr_intern_agent    = "#D69E2E",
  intern_agent       = "#718096",
  judge_agent        = "#9B2C2C"
)

channel_palette <- c(
  comms_huddle    = "#90CDF4",
  one_on_one_chat = "#F6AD55",
  side_huddle     = "#FBD38D",
  official_post   = "#68D391",
  personal_post   = "#FC8181",
  anonymous_post  = "#742A2A"
)

recipient_to_agent <- c(
  legal          = "legal_agent",
  platform_trust = "quality_agent",
  pr             = "pr_agent",
  social_manager = "social_media_agent",
  pr_intern      = "pr_intern_agent",
  intern         = "intern_agent",
  judge          = "judge_agent"
)

embargo_expiry <- ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00")

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
5.2 Communication volume across the 23 rounds
The bar chart below shows the total number of messages produced in each of the 23 rounds. Each round is plotted as one evenly spaced bar because the rounds are not evenly spaced in calendar time. The early rounds are spread across May at one per day while rounds 14 to 23 all fall on 5 June one hour apart. The axis labels show the date and time of each round so the concentration on the expiry day stays visible. The dashed red line marks the embargo expiry at 18:00H on 5 June 2046.


Show the code
vol_data <- messages_tbl |>
  count(round_idx, round_hour_dt) |>
  mutate(round_label = format(round_hour_dt, "%b %d %H:%M"))

ggplot(vol_data, aes(x = round_idx, y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#4682B4", width = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 22.5, colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = 22.4, y = max(vol_data$n) * 0.95,
           label = "Embargo\nexpiry", colour = "#9B2C2C",
           hjust = 1.1, size = 3.2, lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = vol_data$round_idx,
                     labels = vol_data$round_label,
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.01))) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages per round",
       title = "Communication volume across the 23 rounds",
       subtitle = "One bar per round. The final ten rounds all fall on 5 June, the embargo expiry day.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, vjust = 0.5, hjust = 1, size = 7))









The first 13 rounds, spread across 17 May to 4 June, each carry between 23 and 42 messages. The ten rounds on 5 June run from round 14 at 09:00H to round 23 at 18:00H and carry the heaviest traffic, peaking at 56 messages in the final round. The embargo expiry round and the round immediately before it are the two largest in the dataset. This concentration of activity on the expiry day is the starting point for understanding the breach.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
6.1 Flagging embargo-relevant messages
The embargo_hit flag was computed during data preparation. It marks messages whose content, rationalizing, or deliberating field contains any of the merger keywords (harborcrest, civicloom, merger, embargo, references to 17:00 or 18:00). Filtering to the leak window shows how dominant merger talk became.


Show the code
messages_tbl |>
  filter(period == "Leak window") |>
  count(round_idx, embargo_hit) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = embargo_hit, values_from = n, values_fill = 0,
              names_prefix = "embargo_") |>
  mutate(hit_rate = scales::percent(embargo_TRUE / (embargo_TRUE + embargo_FALSE),
                                    accuracy = 1)) |>
  kable(caption = "Embargo-relevant messages per round in the final three hours")



Embargo-relevant messages per round in the final three hours


round_idx

embargo_TRUE
