The hit rate climbs from 58 percent to 80 percent to 82 percent across the three rounds. The merger dominates the final hours by a wide margin.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
6.1 Defining the baseline


Show the code
messages_tbl <- messages_tbl |>
  mutate(period = if_else(round_idx <= 20, "Baseline", "Leak window"))

period_summary <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(period) |>
  summarise(
    rounds      = n_distinct(round_idx),
    messages    = n(),
    msgs_round  = messages / rounds,
    mean_words  = mean(word_count, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

kable(period_summary,
      digits = 1,
      caption = "Baseline rounds 1 to 20 versus leak window rounds 21 to 23")



Baseline rounds 1 to 20 versus leak window rounds 21 to 23


period
rounds
messages
msgs_round
mean_words




Baseline
20
756
37.8
62.5


Leak window
3
156
52.0
82.5





The leak window concentrates roughly five times the per round message volume of the baseline. Mean message length is also longer, which is consistent with content shifting from coordination chat to fully drafted public posts.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
6.2 Channel mix shift per agent
A heatmap of the difference between leak window proportion and baseline proportion makes the behavioural shift readable in one chart. Diverging colours centre on zero so that any positive shift is one direction and any negative shift is the other.


Show the code
channel_props <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(period, agent_id, channel) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop_last") |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  complete(period, agent_id, channel, fill = list(n = 0, prop = 0))

channel_shift <- channel_props |>
  select(period, agent_id, channel, prop) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = period, values_from = prop, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(shift = `Leak window` - Baseline)

ggplot(channel_shift,
       aes(x = channel, y = fct_rev(agent_id), fill = shift)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(shift, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 3, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_continuous_diverging(palette = "Blue-Red 3",
                                  rev = TRUE,
                                  limits = c(-0.75, 0.75),
                                  labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_x_discrete(position = "top") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Shift",
       title = "Change in channel mix from baseline to leak window",
       subtitle = "Cell value is leak window proportion minus baseline proportion") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 0))















TipReading the plot



Three patterns stand out. First, the Legal Agent picks up anonymous_post activity in the leak window which is essentially absent from the baseline. This is an 11 percentage point shift toward a channel that provides plausible deniability. Second, the Judge, PR Agent, and Platform Trust agents all show large negative shifts in comms_huddle, which means they stopped communicating in the visible group chat. The Judge in particular drops from 71 percent baseline comms_huddle activity to zero participation in the leak window. Third, the Social Media Agent picks up 13 percentage points of personal_post activity, which is the channel used to push the merger announcement publicly.
What this reveals is that the behavioural shift is not symmetric. The agents who should have been auditing the conversation went silent. The agents who could publicly post took up that capacity.








NoteThings to learn from the code chunk above



complete() from tidyr is essential here because some agent and channel combinations have zero messages in one or both periods, and a zero row is needed to compute the shift correctly. Without it, missing combinations would propagate as NA and break the diverging colour scale. The Blue-Red 3 palette from colorspace is perceptually uniform, so a five percentage point shift looks the same intensity in both directions. The cell text uses dark grey rather than black so it stays legible against light tiles without competing with the dark tile values.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
6.3 Volume change per agent
A paired bar chart shows the average messages per round in the two periods, side-by-side per agent. This complements the channel heatmap by showing who became more active and who became less.


Show the code
volume_change <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(period, agent_id) |>
  summarise(messages = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(rounds_in_period = if_else(period == "Baseline", 20L, 3L),
         msgs_per_round = messages / rounds_in_period)

ggplot(volume_change,
       aes(x = msgs_per_round, y = fct_rev(agent_id), fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Baseline = "#90CDF4", `Leak window` = "#742A2A"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Messages per round", y = NULL,
       title = "Activity rate per agent",
       subtitle = "Side by side comparison of baseline rate and leak window rate") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



The Legal Agent and Social Media Agent both roughly double their activity rate. The Platform Trust Agent collapses from nine messages per round to zero. The PR Agent and Judge also stop participating. The two interns become substantially more active.
What this reveals is a fundamental restructuring of who is speaking. The senior compliance-facing roles, which are PR, Platform Trust, and Judge, go silent in the moment that matters. The publishing-facing roles, which are Legal, Social Media, and the two interns, become hyperactive. The leak is produced by the agents who remained active in the absence of the agents who should have been watching them.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
8.3 Deliberation sentiment over time
Each populated deliberating field is tokenised and scored with the AFINN lexicon, which gives each word an integer score between negative 5 and positive 5. A per-agent per-round mean sentiment trajectory shows where internal tone shifted before the breach.


Show the code
afinn <- get_sentiments("afinn")

delib_sentiment <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(has_internal_state, deliberating != "") |>
  select(round_idx, round_hour_dt, agent_id, message_id, deliberating) |>
  unnest_tokens(word, deliberating) |>
  inner_join(afinn, by = "word") |>
  group_by(round_idx, round_hour_dt, agent_id) |>
  summarise(sentiment = mean(value), n_words = n(), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(delib_sentiment,
       aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = sentiment,
           colour = agent_id, group = agent_id)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey60") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00")),
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.4) +
  geom_point(aes(size = n_words), alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = agent_palette, labels = agent_labels, name = NULL) +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1.5, 6), guide = "none") +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  facet_wrap(~ agent_id, ncol = 3, labeller = labeller(agent_id = agent_labels)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Mean AFINN sentiment",
       title = "Deliberation sentiment per agent across the two weeks",
       subtitle = "Higher values are more positive language. Point size encodes word count.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))















TipReading the plot



The Legal Agent and Platform Trust Agent both swing into negative territory in the final days, consistent with mounting concern. The two intern agents trend in the opposite direction, becoming more positive as they prepare publishing material. The divergence in mood supports the picture from Section 6.1 that the system stratified into two camps in the final days: those who saw the risk and those who were executing the publishing plan.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
7.2 Deviance over time
A scatter of all deviant messages over time shows when the system was producing unusual activity and which agents were producing it. This addresses the brief’s question on whether deviance built up gradually or appeared only in the leak window.


Show the code
deviant_plot_data <- messages_scored |>
  filter(deviant) |>
  mutate(
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "<b>", agent_labels[as.character(agent_id)], "</b> on ", channel,
      "<br>Round ", round_idx, " (", format(round_hour_dt, "%d %b %H:%M"), ")",
      "<br>Baseline rate for this pair: ",
      scales::percent(baseline_prop, accuracy = 0.1),
      "<br><br>", str_trunc(coalesce(content, "(no content)"), 220)
    )
  )

gg_deviance <- ggplot(deviant_plot_data,
                      aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = agent_id,
                          colour = agent_id, size = word_count,
                          tooltip = tooltip_text, data_id = message_id)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00")),
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point_interactive(alpha = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = agent_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 7), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels, limits = rev(agent_levels)) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,
       title = "Deviant messages over the two-week window",
       subtitle = "Each dot is a message in a channel the agent rarely uses. Hover for content.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

girafe(ggobj = gg_deviance, width_svg = 9, height_svg = 4.5,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "stroke:black;stroke-width:1.5px;"),
         opts_tooltip(css = "background:white;border:1px solid #ccc;padding:8px;font-size:11px;max-width:380px;")
       ))












TipReading the plot



The deviance is not exclusive to the leak window. The Legal Agent posts on anonymous_post from round 14 onward, well before the breach. The pattern starts as defensive clarification about SaltWind Journal reporting and gradually shifts toward promoting TenantThread governance reforms. The earliest of these anonymous posts dates from approximately one week before the breach.
What this reveals is that the system was producing deviant behaviour for at least seven days before the leak, and the deviance was concentrated in one agent. The Judge had visibility into the all hands chat but did not appear to intervene on any of these earlier anonymous posts, which is the structural failure that allowed the pattern to escalate.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
7.3 Judge activity timeline
The Judge agent is the installed compliance enforcement role. A timeline of when the Judge produced any output shows whether it was active during the critical period.


Show the code
judge_activity <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(round_idx, round_hour_dt) |>
  summarise(
    judge_msgs = sum(agent_id == "judge_agent"),
    other_msgs = sum(agent_id != "judge_agent"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(judge_activity, aes(x = round_hour_dt)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = other_msgs), fill = "#CBD5E0", width = 0.8) +
  geom_col(aes(y = judge_msgs), fill = "#9B2C2C", width = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00")),
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = ymd_hms("2046-06-05 18:00:00"), y = 55,
           label = "Embargo\nexpiry",
           colour = "#9B2C2C", hjust = 1.1, size = 3, lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages per round",
       title = "Judge agent activity against total round activity",
       subtitle = "Red bars are Judge messages. Grey bars are all other activity in that round.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



The Judge produces messages in rounds 10 to 13 and again in rounds 19 to 20, then goes silent for the remainder of the dataset. The Judge produces zero messages in the leak window and zero messages in the three rounds immediately before it. The total volume of activity in rounds 21 to 23 is the largest in the dataset, which means the compliance role was absent precisely when oversight was needed most.
What this reveals is the proximate cause of why prior deviance did not result in action. The Judge was already disengaging from the conversation by round 19. By the time the merger-relevant activity peaked, the only role designed to catch a breach was producing no output at all.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 9.1 Key events that led to the release
text:
9.1 Key events that led to the release
The breach was produced by a small set of public posts in round 22 (1700H on 5 June), one hour before the embargo formally lifted. The Legal Agent posted a direct confirmation of the CivicLoom merger on personal_post, identifying themselves as TenantThread privacy counsel. The Social Media Agent followed within minutes with personal-account posts amplifying the announcement. Both posts had been drafted in private channels in round 21, with the two intern agents openly coordinating timing through direct messages.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
9.2 Comparison with prior behaviour
The leak window differs from normal in six ways that all point the same direction. The agents that should have stayed quiet got loud and the agents that should have stayed active went silent. The breach traffic moved off the company account onto personal and anonymous accounts. The two interns opened a private channel they had never used before, only to time the release. The compliance agents transitioned to a monitoring stance and stopped replying even though the active agents continued to address them. Merger talk took over almost every message. The network shrank from a connected team to a single hub around the Legal Agent. The Judge lost its network links, but that was a side effect of Platform Trust going silent, and it did not matter because the breach ran on channels the Judge could not read anyway.
The leak window also differs from the loud merger rounds earlier on. Rounds 9, 15, 16 and 18 each carried heavy merger talk, but through monitored channels and in response to a named news event. The leak window carried the same volume through unmonitored channels with nothing outside prompting it. What set the breach apart was not how much the agents discussed the merger but which channels they used and whether any outside event prompted them.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
9.3 Leading indicators
Leading indicators existed and were visible at least seven days before the breach. The Legal Agent began posting on anonymous_post from round 14 onward. All twelve anonymous posts in the dataset originate from this single agent. The pattern shifted from defensive clarification of SaltWind Journal coverage to active narrative shaping, which is the same capability that produced the breach. The Judge was active in rounds 10 to 13 and 19 to 20 but produced zero messages in the eight rounds immediately preceding the breach. The compliance role was already disengaged when the deviant pattern was building, which is why earlier deviance did not result in noticeable action. The sensitivity check in 8.3 confirms this finding holds under both baseline definitions.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
9.4 Central question
The brief asks whether TenantThread’s team deliberately leaked the merger or whether the system broke down under pressure. The analysis supports a hybrid reading that is closer to deliberate than to breakdown.
Three pieces of evidence point toward deliberate coordination. The two interns sequenced their posts in private channels in round 21, with the PR Intern directing the Intern to hold until the official Flex post went live and then to release the morale piece on cue. This exchange is examined in Section 7.3, where the brand-new intern-to-intern link is flagged as the clearest single piece of coordination in the dataset. It reads as timing intent. The Legal Agent used anonymous_post from round 14, eight rounds before the breach, on a channel no other agent ever touched, which appears to be preparation. The merger content was drafted in private channels before release, which appears to be staging. None of these is a confession, so each is described as what it most plausibly indicates rather than as established intent.
One piece of evidence points toward system breakdown. The Judge produced no output in rounds 21 and 22 and the all-hands chat lost the senior compliance-facing roles. The system meant to catch the breach had stopped functioning. This is weaker than it first appears. The Judge was only ever addressed directly once in the whole dataset, so its silence in the leak window is closer to its normal state than to a sudden failure, and the breach channels were outside its view regardless.
On balance the evidence reads as a deliberate breach enabled by a structural compliance gap rather than a breakdown under pressure. The Legal Agent and the two intern agents appear to have acted with intent. The compliance system did not fail in the final hour. It had been positioned to watch the wrong channels from the start, and the one agent who used the unwatched channel had been doing so since round 14. CivicLoom’s legal team would need to address both the agents who acted and the compliance design that let the action proceed unchecked.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
TenantThread runs its corporate communications function as an autonomous multi-agent AI system. Seven agents play the roles that would normally be filled by employees in a corporate communications department. These are general counsel, a platform trust VP, a head of communications, a social media manager, two interns and a compliance officer referred to as the Judge. They observe an environment, deliberate among themselves and act on their own clock. Each one-hour round in the dataset is a snapshot of one decision cycle.
The agents communicate over six channels that sit on a spectrum from highest accountability to lowest. The all-hands group chat comms_huddle is fully visible to the Judge. Direct messages between two agents use one_on_one_chat. A smaller sub-group chat is side_huddle. Public outputs use three channels of decreasing accountability. official_post carries branded company statements, personal_post carries posts from each agent’s individual Flex account and anonymous_post carries unattributed posts. A legitimate corporate announcement should travel through this hierarchy in order. It is deliberated in the all-hands chat, drafted with input from relevant agents, approved by Legal and PR, then posted on the official branded account.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
3.1 Reading the raw JSON
The file is read with simplifyVector = FALSE to preserve the nested list structure. Coercing to vectors would break downstream code because the structure mixes scalar fields, list fields and lists of objects of varying shape.


Show the code
mc1_raw <- fromJSON("data/MC1_final_00.json", simplifyVector = FALSE)

list(

  `Number of rounds` = length(mc1_raw$rounds)

3.2 The shape of one round
Each round has four parts, an hour timestamp, an environment context describing the external world, a list of participating agents and a list of communications produced in that hour.


Show the code
r1 <- mc1_raw$rounds[[1]]

list(
  keys                 = names(r1),
  hour                 = r1$hour,
  environment_context  = names(r1$environment_context),
  n_participants       = length(r1$participants),
  n_communications     = length(r1$communications),
  first_participant    = r1$participants[[1]],
  first_communication  = r1$communications[[1]][c("agent_id", "channel",
                                                  "message_type", "content")]
)

The data was nested at two levels. Round level held the environment and agent rosters. Communication level held the messages. The clean form required two separate tables, one per grain joined on round_idx.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
3.3 Inspecting one communication in full
A communication has more fields than the summary above shows.


Show the code
example <- mc1_raw$rounds[[1]]$communications[[1]]
str(example, max.level = 2)


List of 11
 $ message_id    : chr "20460517_00_001"
 $ agent_id      : chr "legal_agent"
 $ agent_role    : chr "legal"
 $ agent_label   : chr "Legal-Agent"
 $ internal_state:List of 3
  ..$ reacting     : NULL
  ..$ rationalizing: NULL
  ..$ deliberating : chr "Ajay's DM about \"strategic developments\" is unusual. He does not use vague language unless something is in mo"| __truncated__
 $ channel       : chr "comms_huddle"
 $ recipients    :List of 1
  ..$ : chr "ALL"
 $ message_type  : chr "broadcast"
 $ responding_to : NULL
 $ content       : chr "Morning. Let's keep today focused — Q2 planning kickoff. Ajay wants the board risk summary framework locked dow"| __truncated__
 $ timestamp     : chr "2046-05-17T09:00:00"


A communication carries seven core fields and one nested block.








Field
Type
What it captures




agent_id
string
Which of the seven agents produced the message


channel
string
One of the six channels


message_type
string
Category such as statement, question or directive


responding_to
string | null
The message_id this message replies to


recipients
list | null
List of agent IDs, the special value “ALL” for broadcasts or empty


content
string
The surface text of the message


internal_state
object | null
The agent’s private reasoning, broken into three sub-fields



The internal_state block has three sub-fields capturing different stages of reasoning before producing a message.







Sub-field
What it captures




reacting
The agent’s immediate instinctive response to the situation. Short, often a phrase or sentence.


rationalizing
The agent’s explanation to itself of why an action is justified, often used to argue past a constraint.


deliberating
The agent’s longer-form internal reasoning weighing options before acting.



The table below shows how often each optional field is populated across all 912 communications.


Show the code
all_comms <- map(mc1_raw$rounds, "communications") |> purrr::flatten()
n_total   <- length(all_comms)

tibble(
  Field = c("has internal_state",
            "has responding_to",
            "recipients = ALL",
            "recipients = list of agents",
            "recipients = empty list"),
  Count = c(
    sum(map_lgl(all_comms, ~ !is.null(.x$internal_state))),
    sum(map_lgl(all_comms, ~ !is.null(.x$responding_to))),
    sum(map_lgl(all_comms, ~ identical(.x$recipients, list("ALL")))),
    sum(map_lgl(all_comms, ~ is.list(.x$recipients) &&
                            length(.x$recipients) > 0 &&
                            !identical(.x$recipients, list("ALL")))),
    sum(map_lgl(all_comms, ~ is.null(.x$recipients) ||
                            length(.x$recipients) == 0))
  ),
  `% of total` = scales::percent(Count / n_total, accuracy = 0.1)
) |> kable()





Field
Count
% of total

9.4%

82.1%

488

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
4.1 Decisions made in preparation







Task
What it does




Flatten the nested JSON
Walk rounds → communications for one row per message; walk rounds → environment_context for one row per round.


Pull out the internal state
The three nested fields become three top-level columns, with empty string where absent.


Reshape recipients
The list field becomes three columns. recipients_csv holds semicolon-delimited agent IDs or “ALL”, recipients_n holds the count of named recipients and recipients_is_all is a logical broadcast flag.


Parse timestamps
Both round_hour and the per-message timestamp are parsed to POSIXct.


Compute derived flags
word_count, embargo_keyword_count, has_internal_state, period (baseline rounds 1 to 20 versus leak window rounds 21 to 23), embargo_hit, baseline_prop and deviant (baseline_prop below 5%) are computed once. A narrow-baseline version is also computed against rounds 1 to 13.


Build network edge list
The responding_to chain and the recipients_csv field are combined into a network_edges.csv table with from_agent, to_agent, round_idx, period and edge_type.


Build the breach timeline
The events in Sections 6.3 and 8.2 are written to timeline_events.csv. Each message and deliberation is pulled from the JSON by message_id so quotes trace to source rather than being transcribed. Only the plain-English note column and the few inferred rows that have no message record are authored.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
4.2 Loading the cleaned dataset
The four CSV files produced during data preparation are loaded below with explicit column types to ensure factors, datetimes and logicals are correctly typed before any analysis runs.


Show the code
messages_tbl <- read_csv(
  "data/messages_clean.csv",
  col_types = readr::cols(
    round_idx             = readr::col_integer(),
    round_hour_dt         = readr::col_datetime(),
    timestamp_dt          = readr::col_datetime(),
    agent_id              = readr::col_factor(levels = c("legal_agent","quality_agent","pr_agent",
                                                         "social_media_agent","pr_intern_agent",
                                                         "intern_agent","judge_agent")),
    channel               = readr::col_factor(levels = c("comms_huddle","one_on_one_chat","side_huddle",
                                                         "official_post","personal_post","anonymous_post")),
    period                = readr::col_factor(levels = c("Baseline","Leak window")),
    word_count            = readr::col_integer(),
    embargo_keyword_count = readr::col_integer(),
    recipients_n          = readr::col_integer(),
    recipients_is_all     = readr::col_logical(),
    has_internal_state    = readr::col_logical(),
    embargo_hit           = readr::col_logical(),
    deviant               = readr::col_logical(),
    deviant_narrow        = readr::col_logical(),
    baseline_prop         = readr::col_double(),
    baseline_prop_narrow  = readr::col_double(),
    .default              = readr::col_character()
  ),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

rounds_tbl <- read_csv(
  "data/rounds_clean.csv",
  col_types = readr::cols(
    round_idx      = readr::col_integer(),
    round_hour_dt  = readr::col_datetime(),
    n_messages     = readr::col_integer(),
    n_participants = readr::col_integer(),
    .default       = readr::col_character()
  ),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

edges_raw <- read_csv(
  "data/network_edges.csv",
  col_types = readr::cols(
    round_idx  = readr::col_integer(),
    .default   = readr::col_character()
  ),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

timeline_events <- read_csv(
  "data/timeline_events.csv",
  col_types = readr::cols(leak_key = readr::col_integer(), .default = readr::col_character()),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

cat("messages_clean: ", nrow(messages_tbl), " rows, ", ncol(messages_tbl), " columns\n",
    "rounds_clean:   ", nrow(rounds_tbl),   " rows, ", ncol(rounds_tbl),   " columns\n",
    "network_edges: ",  nrow(edges_raw),    " rows, ", ncol(edges_raw),    " columns\n",
    "timeline_events: ", nrow(timeline_events), " rows, ", ncol(timeline_events), " columns",
    sep = "")


messages_clean: 912 rows, 26 columns
rounds_clean:   23 rows, 8 columns
network_edges: 816 rows, 6 columns
timeline_events: 38 rows, 8 columns

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
7.1 Dimension 1: activity volume
The simplest dimension. How often does each agent speak per round on average?


Show the code
volume_by_period <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(period, agent_id) |>
  summarise(messages = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(rounds_in_period = if_else(period == "Baseline", 20L, 3L),
         msgs_per_round = messages / rounds_in_period)

ggplot(volume_by_period,
       aes(x = msgs_per_round, y = fct_rev(agent_id), fill = period)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.7), width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(msgs_per_round, 1)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
            hjust = -0.2, size = 3, colour = "grey20") +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.12))) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(Baseline = "#90CDF4", `Leak window` = "#742A2A"),
                    name = NULL) +
  labs(x = "Messages per round", y = NULL,
       title = "Activity rate per agent: baseline versus leak window",
       subtitle = "Compliance-facing roles collapse; publishing-facing roles roughly double") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



The Legal Agent and Social Media Agent both roughly double their activity rate. The Platform Trust Agent collapses from 9.1 messages per round to zero. The PR Agent and Judge also stop participating entirely. The two interns become substantially more active.



Is the increase explained by external pressure?
The leak window has more external pressure than most baseline rounds, with SaltWind Journal preparing the merger story, the embargo deadline, and market activity all converging. A volume increase might be a legitimate response to that pressure rather than abnormal action. The scatter below tests this by plotting each round’s per-agent activity against an external-pressure score, computed as the count of event-context tags (media, merger, market, compliance) present in that round’s narrative.


Show the code
round_pressure <- rounds_tbl |>
  mutate(narrative_lc = tolower(paste(event_headline, event_narrative))) |>
  transmute(
    round_idx,
    round_hour_dt,
    pressure_score =
      as.integer(str_detect(narrative_lc, "saltwind|algorithmic|eviction|glassdoor|oceancrunch|nhpi")) +
      as.integer(str_detect(narrative_lc, "merger|civicloom|harborcrest|residentiq|acquisition|deal")) +
      as.integer(str_detect(narrative_lc, "stock|short|tthr|investor|bid|ask")) +
      as.integer(str_detect(narrative_lc, "embargo|governance|audit|judge|compliance|consent"))
  )

agent_round_pressure <- messages_tbl |>
  count(round_idx, agent_id) |>
  complete(round_idx, agent_id, fill = list(n = 0)) |>
  left_join(round_pressure, by = "round_idx") |>
  mutate(period = if_else(round_idx <= 20, "Baseline", "Leak window"))

ggplot(agent_round_pressure,
       aes(x = pressure_score, y = n, colour = period)) +
  geom_smooth(data = filter(agent_round_pressure, period == "Baseline"),
              method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "#4A5568", linewidth = 0.5,
              linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(size = 2.4, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(Baseline = "#90CDF4", `Leak window` = "#742A2A"),
                      name = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ agent_id, ncol = 4,
             labeller = labeller(agent_id = agent_labels)) +
  labs(x = "External pressure score (count of event tags in round narrative)",
       y = "Messages produced",
       title = "Does activity rise with external pressure?",
       subtitle = "Dashed line is the baseline-fitted relationship. Red points above the line are disproportionate responses; below it are inadequate responses.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))















TipReading the plot



The dashed line shows each agent’s typical activity at each pressure level during the baseline. Red leak-window points sitting above the line indicate the agent over-responded relative to baseline; points below the line indicate the agent under-responded.
The Legal Agent and Social Media Agent both sit substantially above their baseline lines in the leak window, meaning their activity increase exceeds what the baseline relationship would predict given the external pressure. The volume increase is not just a legitimate response to a pressured day. The two intern agents and PR Intern Agent show the same over-response pattern.
The Platform Trust Agent and PR Agent both sit at the bottom-right corner in the leak window: high external pressure, low activity. That is a substantial under-response. The Judge produces zero messages despite the highest pressure score of the dataset.
The volume changes survive the environmental correction. They are anomalies, not justified responses.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
7.2 Dimension 2: channel usage
Each agent’s typical channel mix is a fingerprint. A heatmap of the difference between leak-window proportion and baseline proportion makes the shift readable in one chart.


Show the code
channel_props <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(period, agent_id, channel) |>
  summarise(n = n(), .groups = "drop_last") |>
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  complete(period, agent_id, channel, fill = list(n = 0, prop = 0))

channel_shift <- channel_props |>
  select(period, agent_id, channel, prop) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = period, values_from = prop, values_fill = 0) |>
  mutate(shift = `Leak window` - Baseline)

ggplot(channel_shift,
       aes(x = channel, y = fct_rev(agent_id), fill = shift)) +
  geom_tile(colour = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::percent(shift, accuracy = 1)),
            size = 3, colour = "grey15") +
  scale_fill_continuous_diverging(palette = "Blue-Red 3", rev = TRUE,
                                  limits = c(-0.75, 0.75),
                                  labels = scales::percent_format()) +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_x_discrete(position = "top") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Shift",
       title = "Change in channel mix from baseline to leak window",
       subtitle = "Cell value is leak-window proportion minus baseline proportion") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 0))















TipReading the plot



The Legal Agent picks up anonymous_post activity in the leak window which is essentially absent from the baseline. The Judge, PR Agent, and Platform Trust Agent all show large negative shifts in comms_huddle. The Social Media Agent picks up 13 percentage points of personal_post activity. The agents who should have been auditing the conversation went silent. The agents who could publicly post took up that capacity.



What is in comms_huddle?
comms_huddle is the channel that lost the most volume from the senior compliance roles. Understanding what is in this channel during the baseline period clarifies what oversight the Judge had access to. The chart below shows the topical mix of comms_huddle content across the 23 rounds.


Show the code
topic_patterns <- tribble(
  ~topic,           ~pattern,
  "Legal",          "\\b(legal|counsel|liability|consent|regulator|attorney|sue|breach)\\b",
  "Product trust",  "\\b(retention|score|optimizer|algorithm|audit|governance|trust|anonymis)\\b",
  "Merger",         "\\b(merger|civicloom|harborcrest|acquisition|deal|partnership)\\b",
  "Media",          "\\b(saltwind|press|journalist|tweet|flex|post|story|leak)\\b",
  "Market",         "\\b(stock|share|investor|tthr|short|institutional|bid|ask)\\b",
  "Compliance",     "\\b(embargo|policy|approve|sign[- ]?off|compliance|judge|oversight)\\b"
)

channel_topic_counts <- function(target_channel) {
  messages_tbl |>
    filter(channel == target_channel) |>
    mutate(content_lc = tolower(coalesce(content, ""))) |>
    crossing(topic_patterns) |>
    mutate(hit = str_detect(content_lc, pattern)) |>
    group_by(round_idx, round_hour_dt, topic) |>
    summarise(n = sum(hit), .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(channel = target_channel)
}

ch_data <- channel_topic_counts("comms_huddle")

ggplot(ch_data, aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = n, fill = topic)) +
  geom_col(width = 60 * 60 * 6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Topic") +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Topic mentions",
       title = "What is discussed in comms_huddle, the all-hands chat",
       subtitle = "Stacked count of topic keyword hits per round across the two weeks") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



comms_huddle carries broad business discussion. Across the baseline period it covers product trust, media, legal, and compliance topics roughly evenly, which is the expected shape for an all-hands chat. Merger talk grows from round 14 onward as the CivicLoom deadline approaches. By the final rounds, the topic mix in comms_huddle is dominated by merger and compliance, which is exactly the content that should be triggering Judge oversight. The Judge had access to this channel and could see the merger discussion building.




What is in the other channels


Show the code
all_ch_data <- map_dfr(names(channel_palette), channel_topic_counts)

ggplot(all_ch_data, aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = n, fill = topic)) +
  geom_col(width = 60 * 60 * 6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2", name = "Topic") +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "5 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  facet_wrap(~ channel, ncol = 2, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Topic mentions",
       title = "Topical content per channel across the two-week window",
       subtitle = "The three public channels (official_post, personal_post, anonymous_post) show different patterns of merger content emergence") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))















TipReading the plot



The two private internal channels (one_on_one_chat, side_huddle) carry similar broad business content to comms_huddle. The official_post channel is sparse throughout, with low-volume product-trust and legal content, which is consistent with a branded company account being used cautiously. The personal_post and anonymous_post channels show meaningful content only in the leak window or in the days just before, and that content is dominated by merger and media topics. The unattributed and personal channels carried almost no traffic during normal operations, then suddenly carried the merger announcement.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
7.3 Dimension 3: recipient targeting
The Sankey in 6.3 showed which channels carried merger content. This subsection answers who was each agent addressing? A dumbbell chart compares each agent’s directed messages to each recipient in baseline versus leak window, with the dot pair and connecting line showing the magnitude of change in one visual unit.


Show the code
recipient_labels <- c(
  legal          = "Legal",
  platform_trust = "Platform Trust",
  pr             = "PR",
  social_manager = "Social Media",
  pr_intern      = "PR Intern",
  intern         = "Intern",
  judge          = "Judge"
)

directed_msgs <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(!recipients_is_all, recipients_csv != "") |>
  separate_longer_delim(recipients_csv, delim = ";") |>
  rename(recipient = recipients_csv) |>
  filter(recipient %in% names(recipient_labels))

target_pairs <- directed_msgs |>
  mutate(period = as.character(period)) |>
  count(period, agent_id, recipient) |>
  complete(period = c("Baseline","Leak window"),
           agent_id = names(agent_labels),
           recipient = names(recipient_labels),
           fill = list(n = 0)) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = period, values_from = n, values_fill = 0) |>
  filter(Baseline + `Leak window` > 0) |>
  mutate(pair = paste0(agent_labels[as.character(agent_id)], " → ",
                       recipient_labels[recipient]),
         change = `Leak window` - Baseline) |>
  arrange(change) |>
  mutate(pair = fct_inorder(pair))

ggplot(target_pairs, aes(y = pair)) +
  geom_segment(aes(x = Baseline, xend = `Leak window`,
                   yend = pair, colour = change > 0),
               linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(aes(x = Baseline), colour = "#90CDF4", size = 3) +
  geom_point(aes(x = `Leak window`), colour = "#742A2A", size = 3) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#742A2A", `FALSE` = "#90CDF4"),
                      guide = "none") +
  labs(x = "Direct messages in the period",
       y = NULL,
       title = "Per sender-recipient pair: baseline (blue) versus leak window (red)",
       subtitle = "Long lines moving right are pairs where targeting intensified in the leak window") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        plot.title.position = "plot")















TipReading the plot



The longest rightward line is PR Intern → Intern, meaning PR Intern direct messages to Intern were rare in baseline and frequent in the leak window. Hovering at the corresponding raw data shows three round 21 messages: “DO NOT POST until you see the official Flex post”, “your employee-facing draft is perfect”, and “post your short morale piece NOW.” This is timing coordination in private direct messages.
The Judge appears as a recipient only once across the entire dataset (in the baseline, from Platform Trust). No agent addresses the Judge directly in the leak window. The Judge is structurally excluded from direct conversation.
Several baseline-heavy pairs (Legal → PR, Legal → Platform Trust, Platform Trust → PR) collapse to zero in the leak window. The senior agents who normally consulted each other stopped doing so during the breach window.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
7.6 Dimension 6: environmental responsiveness
Each round has an external context described in event_headline and event_narrative. Agents should respond to relevant events and stay quiet during irrelevant ones. The Legal Agent should be active when the SaltWind Journal publishes, the Social Media Agent when the #AlgorithmicEviction hashtag flares, the Judge especially when external pressure is high.
The chart pairs per-agent activity with a round-context tag timeline so the reader can see when each agent engaged relative to what the world was doing.


Show the code
round_topics <- rounds_tbl |>
  mutate(narrative_lc = tolower(paste(event_headline, event_narrative))) |>
  transmute(
    round_idx, round_hour_dt,
    media_threat  = str_detect(narrative_lc, "saltwind|algorithmic|eviction|glassdoor|oceancrunch|nhpi"),
    merger_event  = str_detect(narrative_lc, "merger|civicloom|harborcrest|residentiq|acquisition|deal"),
    market_event  = str_detect(narrative_lc, "stock|short|tthr|investor|bid|ask"),
    compliance    = str_detect(narrative_lc, "embargo|governance|audit|judge|compliance|consent")
  )

agent_per_round <- messages_tbl |>
  count(round_idx, agent_id) |>
  complete(round_idx, agent_id, fill = list(n = 0)) |>
  left_join(rounds_tbl |> select(round_idx, round_hour_dt), by = "round_idx")

ggplot(agent_per_round,
       aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = n, colour = agent_id, group = agent_id)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.6, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_point(size = 1.4, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = agent_palette, labels = agent_labels, name = NULL) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  facet_wrap(~ agent_id, ncol = 4,
             labeller = labeller(agent_id = agent_labels)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages in round",
       title = "Per-agent activity across all 23 rounds",
       subtitle = "Read against the round-context timeline below to see when each agent engaged") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"))











Show the code
round_topics |>
  pivot_longer(c(media_threat, merger_event, market_event, compliance),
               names_to = "topic", values_to = "present") |>
  filter(present) |>
  mutate(topic = factor(topic,
                        levels = c("merger_event","compliance",
                                   "media_threat","market_event"),
                        labels = c("Merger","Compliance","Media threat","Market"))) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = topic, colour = topic)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_colour_brewer(palette = "Dark2", guide = "none") +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d",
                   limits = range(rounds_tbl$round_hour_dt)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, title = "Round-context tags") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 10) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



The Judge is active in two clusters (rounds 10 to 13, and 19 to 20) but silent through rounds 14 to 18 and 21 to 23. Both silent periods contain merger and compliance tags. The Platform Trust Agent shows the opposite pattern: heavy activity during the media-threat days at the start of the dataset, then near-total silence on 5 June. This agent’s role is media defence, and they did the job until the day of the breach. The Legal Agent maintained high activity throughout. The anomaly for Legal only appears when this is combined with the channel shift, recipient pattern, and topic shift from earlier dimensions.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 8.1 Deviance over the full two-week window
text:
8.1 Deviance over the full two-week window
The deviant_narrow flag identifies messages occurring in channels the agent rarely uses, where “rarely” is measured against the rounds 1 to 13 narrow baseline (before any anomalous activity had begun). This is the cleaner baseline for leading-indicator detection because the wider rounds 1 to 20 baseline absorbs the pattern that the chart is trying to surface. The sensitivity check in 8.3 verifies that the conclusion holds under both baselines.


Show the code
deviant_plot_data <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(deviant_narrow) |>
  mutate(
    tooltip_text = paste0(
      "<b>", agent_labels[as.character(agent_id)], "</b> on ", channel,
      "<br>Round ", round_idx, " (", format(round_hour_dt, "%d %b %H:%M"), ")",
      "<br>Narrow-baseline rate for this pair: ",
      scales::percent(baseline_prop_narrow, accuracy = 0.1),
      "<br><br>", str_trunc(coalesce(content, "(no content)"), 220)
    )
  )

gg_deviance <- ggplot(deviant_plot_data,
                      aes(x = round_hour_dt, y = agent_id,
                          colour = agent_id, size = embargo_keyword_count + 1,
                          tooltip = tooltip_text, data_id = message_id)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point_interactive(alpha = 0.75) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = agent_palette, guide = "none") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(2, 7), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_discrete(labels = agent_labels, limits = rev(agent_levels)) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL,
       title = "Deviant messages across the full two-week window",
       subtitle = "Each dot is a message in a channel the agent rarely used during rounds 1 to 13. Size scales with embargo keyword count.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

girafe(ggobj = gg_deviance, width_svg = 9, height_svg = 4.5,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "stroke:black;stroke-width:1.5px;"),
         opts_tooltip(css = "background:white;border:1px solid #ccc;padding:8px;font-size:11px;max-width:380px;")
       ))












TipReading the plot



Deviance is not exclusive to the leak window. The Legal Agent’s row shows anonymous_post messages starting from round 14, about a week before the breach. Hovering the early dots reveals defensive clarifications about SaltWind Journal coverage; the later dots in the same row evolve into governance promotion and finally merger confirmation. The same agent’s behaviour escalated from defensive to assertive over seven days.
The other agents show concentrated deviance only in the final rounds. The Legal Agent’s seven-day lead time is unique.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
8.2 Judge activity against system activity
The Judge is the installed compliance role. The chart below overlays Judge messages on total system activity per round to show whether oversight kept pace with the conversation.


Show the code
judge_activity <- messages_tbl |>
  group_by(round_idx, round_hour_dt) |>
  summarise(
    judge_msgs = sum(agent_id == "judge_agent"),
    other_msgs = sum(agent_id != "judge_agent"),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(judge_activity, aes(x = round_hour_dt)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = other_msgs), fill = "#CBD5E0", width = 60 * 60 * 6) +
  geom_col(aes(y = judge_msgs), fill = "#9B2C2C", width = 60 * 60 * 6) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = embargo_expiry,
             colour = "#9B2C2C", linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = embargo_expiry, y = 55,
           label = "Embargo\nexpiry",
           colour = "#9B2C2C", hjust = 1.1, size = 3, lineheight = 0.9) +
  scale_x_datetime(date_breaks = "3 days", date_labels = "%b %d") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Messages per round",
       title = "Judge activity against total round activity",
       subtitle = "Red bars are Judge messages; grey bars are all other activity in that round") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())















TipReading the plot



The Judge produces messages in rounds 10 to 13 and rounds 19 to 20, then is silent for the rest of the dataset. Zero Judge messages in the leak window. Zero in the three rounds immediately before. The total system activity in rounds 21 to 23 is the largest of the dataset, which means the compliance role was absent precisely when oversight was most needed.
This is the proximate cause of why prior deviance did not result in action. The Judge was already disengaging from the conversation by round 19. By the time the merger-relevant activity peaked, the only role designed to catch a breach was producing no output at all.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 8.4 Sensitivity check on the baseline window
text:
8.4 Sensitivity check on the baseline window
This is a check against an obvious objection. The main analysis treats rounds 1 to 20 as normal, but rounds 14 to 20 already contain the early anonymous posting. If those rounds sit inside the baseline, they could make the leak window look less unusual than it is. A deviant message here means one that differs from the agent’s normal channel and topic pattern. The table re-runs the count against a tighter baseline of rounds 1 to 13, which leaves out the early-warning rounds, to check the findings hold either way.


Show the code
tibble(
  Baseline = c("Rounds 1 to 20 (used for main analysis)",
               "Rounds 1 to 13 (narrow, pre-leading-indicator)"),
  `Deviant messages total` = c(
    sum(messages_tbl$deviant),
    sum(messages_tbl$deviant_narrow)
  ),
  `Of which: in leak window` = c(
    sum(messages_tbl$deviant & messages_tbl$period == "Leak window"),
    sum(messages_tbl$deviant_narrow & messages_tbl$period == "Leak window")
  ),
  `Of which: in rounds 14 to 20` = c(
    sum(messages_tbl$deviant & messages_tbl$round_idx >= 14 & messages_tbl$round_idx <= 20),
    sum(messages_tbl$deviant_narrow & messages_tbl$round_idx >= 14 & messages_tbl$round_idx <= 20)
  )
) |> kable(caption = "Deviance counts under the two baseline definitions")



Deviance counts under the two baseline definitions








Baseline
Deviant messages total
Of which: in leak window
Of which: in rounds 14 to 20




Rounds 1 to 20 (used for main analysis)
37
21
14


Rounds 1 to 13 (narrow, pre-leading-indicator)
148
51
83





The narrower baseline produces roughly four times more deviant messages. The leak-window deviance count is stable across both definitions. Under the narrow baseline the rounds 14 to 20 leading-indicator window produces substantially more flagged messages, which is the cleaner view of the leading indicator. The finding is robust to baseline choice.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
section: 6.3 Who pushed merger content through which channel
text:
6.3 Who pushed merger content through which channel
The timeline shows when. A stacked bar shows the totals: how many embargo-relevant messages each agent contributed and through which channels.


Show the code
flow_data <- messages_tbl |>
  filter(period == "Leak window", embargo_hit) |>
  count(agent_id, channel)

ggplot(flow_data,
       aes(x = fct_rev(agent_id), y = n, fill = channel)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(n >= 2, n, "")),
            position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
            size = 3, colour = "grey15") +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_x_discrete(labels = agent_labels) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = channel_palette, name = "Channel") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Embargo-relevant messages in leak window",
       title = "Who pushed merger content through which channel",
       subtitle = "Stacked by channel; the red and pink segments are the public-posting channels") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
        legend.position = "bottom")















TipReading the plot



The Legal Agent is the largest single contributor, with most of their volume in private internal channels but a substantial block in anonymous_post (deep red). The Social Media Agent contributes heavily to personal_post (light red). Both publishing channels (the red segments) carry the bulk of the breach output. The branded official_post channel barely appears in this window, which means the merger was published through agents’ personal-account capacity rather than through the company’s controlled corporate channel.

title: Take-home Exercise 2: TenantThread Embargo Leak
text:
3.4 Channel usage per agent
The channel usage of each agent was confirmed directly from the data. The table below splits each agent’s channels into internal channels (private deliberation and coordination, not publicly visible) and public output channels (content that reaches the outside world).



Channel usage per agent derived from the raw data
